name: Rome
narrative: Rome sits where the Oostanaula and Etowah meet to form the Coosa, and the same hills and river bottoms that make this town beautiful also make it a serious place to own trees. Elite Catastrophe is based right here in Rome, and Floyd County is our home turf: the historic in-town neighborhoods shaded by oaks that were mature before most of us were born, the ridgetop streets where the wind hits first, and the river-bottom yards where roots sit in damp ground for weeks at a stretch. We climb, trim, and remove trees in this town because we live under the same canopy you do. Older Rome neighborhoods carry a particular kind of tree load. Water oaks and red oaks planted generations ago now stand taller than the houses they shade, with heavy limbs reaching over rooflines, driveways, and sidewalks. Water oaks in particular tend to decline from the inside out, and a trunk that looks solid from the street can be hollow at the base. Add red clay soil that turns greasy after a week of rain, and you get root plates that lose their grip just as the spring thunderstorms arrive. If a leaning pine or a dead limb over the kitchen has been keeping you up at night, that is exactly the call we want to get before the weather makes the decision for you. Our everyday work in Rome is straightforward: full removals in tight yards, careful sectional takedowns where there is no room to drop wood, structural pruning to get limbs off the roof, deadwooding the older oaks worth saving, and stump grinding so the job is actually finished. When severe weather does hit Floyd County, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. Most days, though, the work is quieter than that: keeping Rome's big old trees healthy where they should stay, and taking them down safely where they should not.
treeAngle: mature river-town oaks over historic hillside neighborhoods

name: Lindale
narrative: Lindale grew up around its mill, and the old village streets just south of Rome still show it: modest homes set close together, mature shade trees planted long ago, and yards where one big oak can cover half the lot. That closeness is exactly what makes tree work in Lindale different. When a sweetgum is dying between two houses that sit thirty feet apart, there is no room for guesswork. Limbs come down in pieces, on ropes, in the right order, handled by people who do this every day. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service based just up the road in Rome, which makes Lindale about the shortest trip on our schedule. We know the trees here because they are the same ones we grew up around in Floyd County: water oaks that hollow out with age, sweetgums that shed limbs in summer wind, and tall loblolly pines standing in clay soil that holds water long after the rain stops. When the spring weather turns rough, our crews take storm calls 24/7. Most of what we do in Lindale, though, is planned and scheduled. Removing a dead or declining tree before it picks its own moment to fall. Trimming limbs back off roofs, service drops, and driveways. Grinding out old stumps so you can mow in a straight line again, or finally plant something useful where a tree came down years back. Taking an honest look at a leaner and telling you plainly whether it is a hazard or just a tree with character. If you are in the old village or anywhere on the south side of Floyd County, the process is simple. We walk the yard with you, explain what each tree is doing and why, lay out a clear plan, and leave the place cleaner than we found it. Lindale kept its trees when a lot of towns lost theirs, and helping homeowners live safely under that canopy is work we are glad to have.
treeAngle: old mill village shade trees on close-set lots

name: Cave Spring
narrative: Cave Spring built its whole identity around water, and the same springs and creeks that draw visitors to Rolater Park keep the ground under this town's trees damp far longer than most of Floyd County. About thirteen miles south of Rome, the town holds one of the prettiest old canopies in the county: mature hardwoods shading the historic district, big oaks leaning over the antique shops and side streets, and stands of pine filling the rural edges where the small-lot core gives way to open land. That mineral-rich, spring-fed ground is a mixed blessing for trees. It grows them tall and full, but it also stays soft, and a water oak or loblolly pine with roots sitting in wet clay along Cedar Creek can lose its footing in a hard spring blow. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service based just up the road in Rome, and Cave Spring is one of the shorter runs on our schedule. The work here follows the town's split personality. In the historic core, the job is usually preservation: crown cleaning the grand old hardwoods, reducing weight on limbs that overhang porches and tin roofs, and cutting out deadwood before it drops on a sidewalk. On the rural lots, the work shifts toward removals: the brittle pine near the bedroom wall or the storm-split hardwood along the fence. Stump grinding finishes either one, turning an old removal back into usable yard. When violent weather rolls through this corner of Floyd County, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. The rest of the time, the smart move in Cave Spring is a slow walk around the property with someone who cuts trees every working day.
treeAngle: historic-district hardwoods on damp spring-fed ground

name: Cedartown
narrative: How long has that big oak been standing over your house? In Cedartown, especially on the older streets near downtown, the honest answer is often longer than the house itself. Cedartown is the seat of Polk County, and like most older Georgia county seats it carries a real canopy: red oaks and water oaks over the in-town blocks, sweetgums along the back fences, and stands of loblolly and shortleaf pine ringing the edges of town. Those trees are a big part of why Cedartown feels the way it does. They are also the part of the property most homeowners never think about until something cracks. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service based in Rome, and Polk County is a regular part of our week. The work in Cedartown is familiar: mature hardwoods that need weight taken out of the crown before the summer storm season, pines that have browned out and turned brittle, broken limbs hung up over sheds and fences, and stumps left behind by removals that were never truly finished. The clay soil here does trees no favors either. It gets soft after long rain, and a shallow-rooted pine in wet clay is the classic Northwest Georgia blowdown waiting on a gust. Anyone who has walked or ridden the Silver Comet Trail knows how much of Polk County's character comes from its trees. Our job is to keep the ones on your own property an asset instead of a liability: pruning for structure and clearance, removing hazard trees before they choose their own schedule, and grinding stumps flush so the yard is usable again. When severe weather tears through Polk County, Elite Catastrophe runs storm crews 24/7 until the calls stop coming. The rest of the year, we would much rather look at a tree you are wondering about while it is still standing tall than after it has met your roof.
treeAngle: county seat hardwood canopy with pine-ringed edges

name: Rockmart
narrative: Spring in Northwest Georgia follows a pattern, and Rockmart homeowners know it by heart. A warm, heavy afternoon, a line of storms building to the west, and then twenty violent minutes that rearrange somebody's yard. In Polk County that usually means pine tops snapped out, sweetgum limbs scattered across driveways, and at least one tree leaning somewhere it was not leaning the day before. Tree trouble here rarely gives much notice, which is why the smart time to deal with a questionable tree in Rockmart is while it is still standing straight. Elite Catastrophe covers Rockmart from our home base in Rome, and we work both sides of town. The older streets near downtown hold mature oaks that have shaded the same houses for generations, trees that mostly need maintenance: deadwood removed, weight reduced, limbs brought back off the roofline. The newer subdivisions have a different problem, the leftover loblolly pine. Those pines grew up inside a stand, sheltered and slender, and once construction took away the trees around them they were left standing alone in heavy clay that turns soft when saturated. A tall, exposed pine in wet ground is the tree most likely to end up horizontal after a windstorm, and it is the one we most wish homeowners would ask about beforehand. Our Rockmart work runs the full list: complete removals of dead, dying, or badly placed trees, careful trimming around roofs and service drops, crown cleaning for the big hardwoods worth keeping, honest hazard assessments when you are just not sure, and stump grinding so a removal ends with a usable yard instead of an obstacle. Rockmart's stretch of the Silver Comet Trail shows how good this corner of Polk County looks when trees are where they belong. When one ends up where it does not belong at two in the morning, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. Either way, you get a family-owned crew that treats your yard like it sits on our own street.
treeAngle: storm-snapped pines and leftover subdivision loblollies

name: Aragon
narrative: Aragon is a small former mill town in Polk County, the kind of place where a single big tree can shade most of a modest lot. About twenty miles from Rome, it sits along the Silver Comet Trail corridor near Rockmart, and its yards carry the same tree stock you find all through this part of Georgia: mature hardwoods left over from the mill-town days, plus loblolly and shortleaf pine crowding the edges and the newer parcels. On lots this size, tree work is a close-quarters job. When a leaning pine stands between two houses or a dying sweetgum hangs over a driveway, there is no room to simply drop the wood. Limbs come off in sections, on ropes, in the right order. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service that works Aragon regularly out of our Rome base, and we know these trees because they are the same species we handle at home every week. Water oaks that hollow quietly with age. Pines rooted shallow in red clay that turns loose after a wet stretch. Sweetgums that fling limbs across a yard in a summer gust. Our everyday work here is planned and steady: removing dead or badly placed trees before they fall on their own, trimming limbs back off roofs and service drops, thinning the crowns of the big hardwoods worth keeping, and grinding stumps flush so a mower can pass. If severe weather blows through Polk County, our crews take storm calls 24/7. Most of the time, though, the best call in Aragon is the one made in fair weather, about the tree you have been eyeing since the last storm season.
treeAngle: mill-town hardwoods and pines on modest lots

name: Calhoun
narrative: Calhoun wears two faces, and your trees depend on which one your house sits in. The Gordon County seat has its established side: in-town streets where water oaks and red oaks have had decades to spread over roofs, sidewalks, and power lines. And it has its I-75 side, where newer neighborhoods keep filling in near the interstate and the trees are either fresh plantings or tall pines left over from whatever grew there before the graders arrived. Both kinds of yard end up needing tree work. They just need it for different reasons. The old-canopy problems in Calhoun are the classic ones. Big hardwoods carrying dead limbs over the driveway. Water oaks that look full and green while the trunk quietly rots from the inside. Trees planted a few feet from the foundation back when they were the size of a broomstick. On the newer side, the usual suspect is the leftover loblolly: tall, thin, shallow-rooted in red clay, and suddenly exposed to wind it never had to stand against before. After a wet week that clay loses its grip, and the spring storms do the rest. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service based in Rome, and Gordon County sits comfortably inside our regular working range. We bring the same crew and equipment up to Calhoun that we use at home: removals in tight quarters, precision trimming, deadwood cleanup in mature crowns, hazard evaluations for leaners and storm-scarred trunks, and stump grinding that leaves you a lawn instead of a monument. When severe weather crosses Gordon County, our Elite Catastrophe storm crews respond 24/7. For everything short of that, the easiest peace of mind is a calm walk around your Calhoun property with someone who works on trees every single day: which ones are fine, which ones need attention, and which one should come down before it decides on its own.
treeAngle: old canopy versus corridor-growth pines off I-75

name: Resaca
narrative: Along the Oostanaula River in Gordon County, Resaca is a tiny town wrapped in wooded river-bottom land, and that low, damp ground shapes everything about its trees. The river and its floodplain keep the clay soft for weeks at a stretch, which grows big timber but also gives roots a poor grip when the spring storms arrive. About eighteen miles from Rome and split by the I-75 corridor, Resaca has more woods than houses, and many of its trees stand close to homes, barns, and the old battlefield ground that runs through the area. Elite Catastrophe, a family-owned crew, covers Resaca on a regular schedule from our Rome base, and the river-bottom setting turns up a particular kind of tree trouble here. Tall loblolly and shortleaf pines that uproot cleanly when saturated ground lets go. Water oaks along the banks that hollow from the inside while looking healthy up top. Sweetgums and red oaks with heavy limbs stretched out over roofs and outbuildings. Our work in Resaca is the everyday list handled with care: removing hazard trees before the next storm chooses for you, reducing weight in crowns that have outgrown their footing, pruning limbs back off structures, and grinding stumps so a finished removal leaves usable ground. Because so many properties here sit on acreage, we also spend time simply walking the tree line with owners, pointing out which trees near the house are sound and which are quietly declining. When severe weather crosses this stretch of Gordon County, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. The better path is dealing with the leaning riverside pine now, on a calm afternoon, rather than after it has already come down.
treeAngle: river-bottom pines and hollowing bank oaks

name: Summerville
narrative: Up in Chattooga County the land starts folding toward the Lookout Mountain foothills, and that terrain changes how trees live and how they fail. Summerville sits among wooded ridges, and wind behaves differently here than it does out in flat country. Storms funnel along the valleys, gusts pour over the ridgelines, and the tall pines and slope-side hardwoods around town take the brunt of it. Anyone who has been through a few spring storm seasons in Summerville has watched a healthy-looking tree lose half its crown in a single afternoon. Elite Catastrophe makes the drive up from Rome regularly, because Chattooga County homeowners live with some of the most storm-tested trees in our whole working area. The species list is familiar Northwest Georgia: loblolly and shortleaf pines that snap or uproot in saturated clay, water oaks that hollow with age, red oaks with heavy horizontal limbs, and sweetgums that drop wood in every stiff wind. What the foothills add is exposure. A tree on a slope or a ridge shoulder catches more wind, anchors in thinner soil, and leans harder as it grows toward the light. That lean is often the first thing a homeowner notices and the last thing they should ignore. Our Summerville work leans toward hazard management for exactly that reason: removing dead and declining trees before they come down on their own terms, reducing weight in crowns that have outgrown their root systems, cutting limbs back off rooflines, and grinding stumps so old removals stop being obstacles in the lawn. We are a family-owned outfit, and we treat a yard in Summerville the way we treat one on our own street in Rome. When the weather turns genuinely dangerous in Chattooga County, Elite Catastrophe fields storm calls 24/7. The better plan is the boring one: handle the questionable tree this month, not on the night the radar turns red.
treeAngle: wind-exposed foothill ridges and valley storm funnels

name: Trion
narrative: Tucked in the Chattooga River valley beneath the Lookout Mountain foothills, Trion is a mill town of tight village lots where the wind is the deciding factor for trees. The valley funnels gusts along its length, and a storm that would merely rattle a yard out in flat country can strip a crown here in minutes. Roughly twenty-eight miles north of Rome, Trion packs its houses close together the way old mill villages do, so a full-grown tree often stands within reach of two or three roofs at once. That combination, tight lots plus valley wind, makes careful takedown work essential. There is rarely room to fell a tree whole, so limbs and trunk sections come down on ropes, in order, with the drop zone controlled every step. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned crew that drives up to Trion regularly from Rome, and the trees here are the storm-tested Northwest Georgia set: loblolly and shortleaf pine that snap or pull out of wet clay, water oaks gone hollow with age, red oaks holding heavy horizontal limbs, and sweetgums that shed wood in every hard blow. Wind exposure is what pushes an ordinary lean into a genuine hazard, and a leaning tree beside a mill-village house is exactly the kind of call we would rather get early. Our Trion work runs the usual list: hazard removals, crown reduction on trees that catch too much wind, clearance pruning off rooflines, and stump grinding to reclaim the tight yard space. When the valley weather turns violent, Elite Catastrophe fields storm calls 24/7. For everything short of that, a calm-day walk-through is the surest way to settle the question before the next storm season.
treeAngle: valley-wind exposure on tight mill-village lots

name: Cartersville
narrative: Red oaks, water oaks, sweetgums, and tall loblolly pines: Cartersville yards hold the full Northwest Georgia lineup, sometimes all four on a single lot. As the largest city in Bartow County, sitting on the I-75 corridor between Atlanta and Chattanooga, Cartersville has every kind of neighborhood, which means every kind of tree situation. Established streets near the center of town carry heavy mature canopy, with hardwood crowns spreading over roofs that were built long after the trees were planted. Newer subdivisions farther out show the opposite profile: young ornamental trees in the front yard and a wall of leftover pines along the back fence, rooted in clay that was graded and compacted during construction. Both situations fail in predictable ways. Mature water oaks decline from the inside, shedding big limbs without warning on calm days and coming apart entirely in storms. Sweetgums drop wood constantly and split at weak unions. Construction-stressed pines, their roots cut or buried during grading, can look fine for several summers, then brown out, turn brittle, and quietly become the most dangerous tree on the street. Elite Catastrophe covers Cartersville from our home base in Rome with the same family-owned crew that works our own neighborhoods. The everyday services are the heart of it: removing dead, dying, and badly placed trees, trimming limbs off rooflines and away from driveways, thinning and cleaning the big crowns worth preserving, giving honest evaluations of leaners and storm-scarred trunks, and grinding stumps below grade so the lawn goes back to being a lawn. When a storm system rakes across Bartow County, our crews answer emergency calls 24/7 until the last hazard is on the ground. If there is a Cartersville tree you have been watching with one eye all season, the time to get a straight answer about it is now, while every option is still on the table.
treeAngle: full-lineup lots from mature canopy to construction-stressed pines

name: Adairsville
narrative: Small towns keep their oldest trees, and Adairsville is proof. Drive through the historic center of this north Bartow County town and the shade is doing half the work: mature oaks over old house lots, big sweetgums along the property lines, cedars and pines filling in behind them. Trees like that are part of why people choose a place like Adairsville over a bigger city down the interstate. But a tree that has stood beside a house for generations has also spent that time collecting deadwood, storm scars, and the kind of interior decay nobody can see from the porch. Elite Catastrophe works Adairsville and the surrounding north Bartow countryside from our base in Rome, and the route between the two is one we know well. What we find here is a mix. In-town lots often have a single huge oak that dominates everything around it. Farmhouse properties carry lines of old hardwoods along drives and fencerows. Newer homes on the edges of town come with young landscaping out front and a few tall leftover pines out back. Each situation calls for its own plan. The huge oak may need careful crown cleaning and weight reduction rather than removal. The brittle pine standing close to a bedroom wall usually needs to go. The stump from a tree that came down years ago needs grinding before it spends another season attracting insects and dulling mower blades. Our crews handle removals, trimming, hazard assessments, and stump grinding in Adairsville with the same care we bring to our home county. When violent weather crosses Bartow County, we take storm calls 24/7. And for everything else, the invitation is simple: ask us to walk the property with you. Ten minutes of looking up together, with someone who climbs and cuts trees every working day, usually answers the question you have been putting off since the last big storm blew through.
treeAngle: generations-old small town oaks and farmhouse hardwoods

name: Emerson
narrative: Growth is the defining fact of Emerson right now, and growth is exactly what changes a town's trees. This small Bartow County town sits near Lake Allatoona and the LakePoint complex along the I-75 corridor, roughly twenty-eight miles from Rome, and it has been filling in fast with new development beside its older lots. That mix is what we see in the yards. Established parcels still hold mature hardwoods, oaks and sweetgums that have shaded the same ground for decades. The newer subdivisions tell a different story: tall loblolly pines left standing after construction stripped away the trees around them, now rooted in compacted clay and suddenly exposed to wind on all sides. A pine that grew up sheltered inside a stand, then lost its neighbors when the lots around it were built, is the single tree most likely to end up flat after a storm, and Emerson has plenty of them. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service that covers Emerson from our Rome base, bringing the same crew and gear we use in our own neighborhoods. The everyday work here is steady and planned: removing dead, dying, or dangerously placed trees, thinning and cleaning the crowns of hardwoods worth keeping, cutting limbs back off new rooflines, evaluating the leftover pines honestly, and grinding stumps below grade so a new lawn can go in clean. Lakeside lots add their own wrinkle, with damp ground near the water keeping roots loose long after a rain. When storms roll off the lake and across Bartow County, Elite Catastrophe answers emergency calls 24/7. Short of that, the best time to look hard at a questionable pine is now, before the next line of weather does it for you.
treeAngle: construction-exposed pines in a fast-growing lake town

name: Euharlee
narrative: The covered bridge over Euharlee Creek is what most people picture when they think of this Bartow County town, but the trees around it are just as much a part of the setting. Euharlee blends rural and suburban, with wooded creek bottoms, older home places under mature hardwoods, and newer neighborhoods edged by pine. About twenty-six miles from Rome, the town keeps the same tree character as the rest of Northwest Georgia: red oaks with heavy horizontal limbs, water oaks that hollow out from the inside as they age, sweetgums that drop wood in every strong wind, and loblolly pines standing shallow-rooted in red clay. The creek bottoms are where that clay stays wettest, and a pine or bankside oak in soaked ground can lose its footing when a spring storm leans on it. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service that works Euharlee on a regular schedule from our Rome base. The work sorts into two kinds. Around the historic core and the older properties, it tends toward preservation: crown cleaning, weight reduction, and deadwood removal on the big hardwoods worth keeping. In the newer neighborhoods and out on the acreage, it leans toward removals and hazard cleanup: the brittle pine near the house or the storm-damaged limb hanging over the drive. Stump grinding closes out either kind of job so the ground goes back to lawn. When severe weather crosses this part of Bartow County, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. Otherwise, the smartest move in Euharlee is the unhurried one: have someone who cuts trees every working day walk the property and tell you plainly which ones are sound and which need attention.
treeAngle: creek-bottom hardwoods and pines by the covered bridge

name: Canton
narrative: The pattern is familiar across North Georgia. A hot afternoon, a wall of storms building to the west, then a violent stretch of wind and hail that leaves Canton homeowners staring at a tree where a tree should not be. As the seat of Cherokee County, sitting on the Etowah River amid wooded hills, Canton mixes a historic downtown with rings of newer subdivisions, and both carry serious tree load. The older streets hold mature oaks that have long spread over the rooftops. The newer neighborhoods were carved out of pine stands, leaving tall loblollies rooted in red clay that turns soft after a long rain. When a storm hits Canton, those are the trees that fail: pines that snap or uproot, big hardwoods that drop limbs or split, and leaners that end up leaning on a house. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Canton and the rest of Cherokee County. Storm response is where we lead, and when severe weather crosses the county our emergency crews work structures first, meaning the trees that have landed on homes, cars, and roads come before anything cosmetic. That line is answered around the clock, because a tree through the roof at two in the morning cannot wait for business hours. When the roof itself is opened up, we tarp it to keep the weather out until repairs can follow. The storm is the headline, but the everyday work is available year round: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and cleanup of whatever a storm leaves behind. Wondering about a leaning pine on a calm afternoon in Canton? That is a call worth making before the sky turns green.
treeAngle: county seat canopy over Etowah River subdivisions

name: Woodstock
narrative: Woodstock grew from a small railroad town into one of the largest suburbs in Cherokee County, and the older sections kept their trees the whole way. Heavy hardwood canopy shades the established neighborhoods, while the fast-growing subdivisions around them hold the tall pines left behind when construction cleared the trees that once sheltered them. That is a lot of wood over a lot of rooftops, and North Georgia storms know how to find it. When straight-line winds and hail move through, the failures come in a hurry: a loblolly uprooted out of saturated clay and laid across a driveway, an oak limb punched through a garage, a whole tree leaned onto the power line. That is when Elite Catastrophe gets the call. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Woodstock and the surrounding Cherokee County. Storm response leads what we do here, handled structures first: the trees that have hit homes and vehicles or blocked roads come first, in the order that gets people safe and moving again. Those emergency calls are answered around the clock and year round, through the night and through the weekend, because that is when the wind does its damage. If a falling tree opens the roof, we tarp it so the next band of rain stays outside. Once the emergency passes, the same crew handles the everyday list any time you need it: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and hauling off what the storm cracked or killed. The storm is what most people call about in Woodstock, but a questionable tree over your roof on a calm week is reason enough too.
treeAngle: heavy leftover canopy over fast-growing wooded subdivisions

name: Holly Springs
narrative: Follow I-575 north through Cherokee County and Holly Springs is the growing town on either side of it, filling in with wooded neighborhoods where pines and hardwoods stand tight against new homes. That growth is why storm season matters so much here. Every subdivision carved into the woods leaves tall trees behind, and tall trees in saturated red clay are exactly what North Georgia's straight-line winds go after. The storm math is unforgiving. A loblolly pine that grew up sheltered inside a stand is left tall and exposed once construction takes away its neighbors, and after a week of rain its roots lose their grip in soft clay. One hard gust and it is across the driveway, on the roof, or hung up on the service line. Big oaks fail differently, shedding heavy limbs or splitting at weak unions, but the result on the ground looks the same. Elite Catastrophe answers those calls as a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Holly Springs and the rest of Cherokee County. Storm response is our lead work, run structures first: the trees down on houses, cars, and roads clear before anything else, with emergency service around the clock. When a limb or trunk punches through the roof, we tarp it to hold back the weather until repairs happen. The storm is the headline along the I-575 corridor, but the full list stays available year round: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and storm damage cleanup once the pressure is off. A tree you have been eyeing since the last blow is worth a look before the next one arrives.
treeAngle: I-575 corridor pines over growing wooded neighborhoods

name: Ball Ground
narrative: Up in the north end of Cherokee County, where the land starts to climb toward the mountains, Ball Ground keeps the feel of a small historic town wrapped in woods. The trees here are a mix of tall pines and mature hardwoods, many of them on wooded acreage and ridge-edge lots where the wind has a clear run at them. That exposure changes how storms play out. When a North Georgia system comes through with straight-line winds and hail, a tree on a slope or an open ridge shoulder catches far more force than one tucked into town, and saturated red clay does the rest, letting a loblolly's roots slip until the whole tree goes over. What lands is the emergency: a pine dropped across the driveway, a hardwood limb through the roof, a trunk leaned onto the power line with the lights out behind it. That is the work Elite Catastrophe answers in Ball Ground. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Ball Ground and the north end of Cherokee County, and storm response is where we lead. Our crews run structures first, clearing trees off homes, vehicles, and roads ahead of anything else, with emergency calls answered around the clock and year round. If the roof is opened up in the process, we tarp it to keep the rain out. Once the hazards are down, the same crew handles the ordinary work whenever it suits you: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and cleanup of storm-damaged wood. In a place as wooded and wind-exposed as Ball Ground, the storm call earns its keep, but a leaning tree on a quiet day is just as welcome.
treeAngle: north Cherokee hardwoods and pines near the mountains

name: Waleska
narrative: Waleska is a small, rural town in Cherokee County, best known as the home of Reinhardt University and wrapped in the kind of wooded, open acreage that gives big trees plenty of room to grow. That same room means a lot of tall timber standing close to homes, barns, and drives, and when a storm hits Waleska, all that wood becomes the problem. The failures follow the regional script. Loblolly pines, shallow-rooted in red clay that softens after days of rain, snap off or heave out of the ground when straight-line winds hit. Old oaks and other hardwoods shed heavy limbs or come apart at the trunk. On rural lots the fallen tree often takes a fence, a shed, or a stretch of driveway with it, and sometimes the power line goes down under the weight. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Waleska and the rest of Cherokee County. Storm response leads the way, and structures first is how we work: trees off houses, vehicles, and roads before anything else, with emergency calls answered around the clock and year round because storms here do not keep office hours. When a tree tears open the roof, we tarp it to keep the inside dry until repairs can be made. After the urgent work, the everyday services stay available on your schedule: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and storm damage cleanup so the property is usable again. Waleska's headline is storm response, but the same crew is glad to look at a tree on an ordinary afternoon, long before the radar lights up.
treeAngle: rural wooded acreage around Reinhardt University

name: Nelson
narrative: Nelson sits right on the Cherokee and Pickens county line, a small town in old marble country where the surroundings are rural and heavily wooded. It is a quiet place most of the year. Then North Georgia's storm season arrives, and the quiet gets interrupted by the sound of trees coming down. The setup around Nelson is all tall timber and open ground. Loblolly pines stand in red clay that turns soft after a long soaking rain, and when straight-line winds and hail push through, those pines snap or lift out at the roots. Mature hardwoods fail too, dropping big limbs or splitting where the trunk forks. On these rural properties a falling tree tends to find something: a roofline, a vehicle, a driveway, or the power line running out to the house. When that happens, Elite Catastrophe answers. We are a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Nelson and the north end of Cherokee County. Storm response is our lead work, run structures first: the trees down on homes, cars, and roads come off ahead of anything else, and emergency calls are answered around the clock and year round. If the roof is punctured, we tarp it to hold back the next round of weather. Once the immediate danger is cleared, the same crew takes care of the rest whenever it suits you: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and cleanup of the wood a storm leaves behind. In a rural, wooded town like Nelson the storm is the reason most people call, but a declining tree near the house is worth handling on a calm day, well before it picks its own moment.
treeAngle: rural wooded lots in old marble country

name: Marietta
narrative: In Marietta, the call often comes right after the sky has finished its worst. A summer storm pushes through Cobb County, and one of the big oaks that has shaded a street near the historic square for years finally lets go, landing across a roof or splitting a driveway. That is the moment Elite Catastrophe moves for. We are a family-owned tree and storm-response crew based up in Rome, and we serve Marietta year round, with the storm emergency as the reason most people first call. When a storm hits Marietta, our work is triage before anything else. Structures come first: the tree through the roof, the limb pinning a car in the drive, the trunk blocking the only way in or out. If the roof is opened up, we can tarp it to hold back the next round of weather until storm roof repair can follow. Marietta's older neighborhoods carry some of the densest mature canopy in Cobb. Water oaks and red oaks planted decades ago now stand taller than the houses they shelter, with heavy limbs reaching over rooflines and power lines, and when straight-line winds and hail work them over the failures come fast. None of that is only a storm-day service, though. The everyday tree work is available around the clock and year round: full removals of trees too far gone to save, trimming broken or overgrown limbs back to safe wood, storm damage cleanup, and grinding out the stumps a takedown leaves behind. Whether the radar is red over Cobb County or the sky is clear, Elite Catastrophe answers calls in Marietta and points the trucks toward town.
treeAngle: storm-hit mature oaks near the historic square

name: Kennesaw
narrative: Kennesaw grew up in the shadow of its mountain, and the wooded subdivisions filling in around it are heavy with trees. That is beautiful most of the year and a genuine problem for about twenty minutes at a time, when a North Georgia storm line comes through and the wind funnels across the ridges. Elite Catastrophe is a Rome-based, family-owned crew serving Kennesaw, and when a storm hits this part of Cobb County we answer the phone at two in the morning and get on the road. The tree profile in Kennesaw's newer neighborhoods is the classic setup for wind damage. Tall pines that grew up sheltered inside a stand, then were left standing alone after construction cleared the trees around them, now rise exposed in compacted red clay. Add a saturated spring and a hard gust, and those are the pines most likely to end up horizontal, sometimes through a bedroom. Mixed in are maturing hardwoods over the older parts of town that shed heavy limbs under the same conditions. When we roll into Kennesaw after a storm, structures come first: trees on houses, trees on cars, trees blocking a road or a driveway, with roof tarping on hand when a limb has punched through. That storm response is the headline, but it is not the whole service. Removals, tree trimming, storm damage cleanup, and stump grinding are available around the clock and year round, storm or calm, for any homeowner who wants a hazard handled before the weather decides it. The wind-prone trees below Kennesaw Mountain do not wait for a convenient day, and neither do we.
treeAngle: wind-exposed subdivision pines below Kennesaw Mountain

name: Acworth
narrative: Acworth is the shortest drive from our Rome base of any Cobb County city, which matters most on the night a storm has just torn through. This is lake country, wrapped around Lake Acworth and Lake Allatoona, and the wooded lots that slope toward the water are thick with tall trees. When a spring or summer storm crosses the lake and slams into that canopy, the damage can be sudden and severe. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned crew that serves Acworth, and being the closest Cobb city to us simply means we can usually reach a storm here faster. Lakeside lots bring their own hazards. Trees on a slope anchor in thinner, wetter soil, they lean toward the open water and the light, and when the wind pours across the lake they catch the full force of it. Loblolly pines uproot out of saturated red clay, mature oaks lose big limbs, and what falls tends to fall toward the house at the top of the lot. Our storm response in Acworth is structures-first every time: the tree on the roof, the limb through a window, the trunk across the only driveway down to the water. When a roof takes a hit we can tarp it against the next band of weather. That urgent work leads, but it does not stand alone. Tree removal, tree trimming, storm damage cleanup, and stump grinding are all available around the clock and year round, so an Acworth homeowner can take down a leaning lakeside pine on a calm afternoon instead of waiting for the storm to make the call. When Cobb County weather turns dangerous, Elite Catastrophe runs those calls anytime.
treeAngle: wooded lakeside lots on Lake Acworth slopes

name: Smyrna
narrative: Smyrna packs a lot of house and a lot of tree into a small footprint. The Jonquil City is one of the denser inner suburbs of Cobb County, with mature street trees lined up close over sidewalks, driveways, and rooftops that sit only a few feet apart. That closeness is exactly what makes storm damage here so unforgiving: when a tree comes down in Smyrna, there is rarely open ground for it to land on, so it lands on something built. Elite Catastrophe serves Smyrna from our Rome base, and we make the drive because a family in a tight neighborhood with a tree through the roof needs a crew that does this work day in and day out. The mature street trees that give Smyrna its character are also the ones that fail into houses. Older water oaks and red oaks carry heavy limbs over rooflines, and when hail and straight-line winds work them over, or the red clay softens under a week of rain, big wood comes down in the worst possible place. Taking trees off structures in a dense neighborhood is precision work: limbs come down in pieces, in the right order, with no room for error between two close houses. When a storm hits Smyrna we handle the emergency first, tarp any opened roof to keep the rain out, then clear the storm damage. And the calmer work is here on the same schedule, available around the clock and year round: tree removal, tree trimming, and stump grinding for the leaning oak or the crowded pine you would rather deal with today than during the next warning. When the radar turns red over Cobb, Elite Catastrophe answers, and it answers on the quiet days too.
treeAngle: close-set mature street trees over tight lots

name: Powder Springs
narrative: Powder Springs is growing fast, and much of that growth happened right in among the trees. New neighborhoods here are carved out of wooded ground and set into the heavy red clay that defines this corner of Cobb County, and that combination is what puts the town on watch every spring. Saturated clay loses its grip on a root plate, a straight-line wind leans on an exposed tree, and something that stood straight yesterday is on the house today. Elite Catastrophe is a Rome-based, family-owned company serving Powder Springs, and the storm is what most people are calling about when they first reach us. The trees that cause the most trouble in these newer subdivisions are the leftover pines. They grew up crowded and slender inside a stand, then construction took away the trees around them and left them tall, thin, and exposed, rooted in clay that was graded and packed hard. A wet week plus a hard gust, and those are the pines that snap or uproot onto a roof. When a storm hits Powder Springs, structures come first, always: the tree on the house, the limb over the front door, the trunk blocking the drive, with roof tarping and storm damage cleanup right behind. That is the urgent lane, and it leads for a reason. But the full service runs year round and around the clock: tree removal of what is too far gone, tree trimming to bring broken or overgrown limbs back to safe wood, and stump grinding so the yard is usable again. Storm or clear sky, the crew that answers is the same, and Powder Springs can call it anytime.
treeAngle: storm-toppled leftover pines in red-clay subdivisions

name: Austell
narrative: When a storm parks over Austell, the town gets hit two ways at once. Wind and hail work the trees while Sweetwater Creek and the low bottoms take on water, and a tree standing in soft, saturated ground near the creek does not need much of a gust to come over. Austell mixes an older town core with newer growth on the edges, so the hazards run the full range. Elite Catastrophe serves Austell from Rome, a family-owned crew that answers the call and drives to the damage when severe weather turns those trees into emergencies. The flood-prone bottoms are what set Austell apart. Ground that stays wet for days holds trees loosely, and the loblolly pines and creek-side hardwoods rooted in it are prime candidates to uproot when the wind arrives on top of the water. Up in the older neighborhoods, mature oaks over close-set houses fail the way old oaks do, shedding heavy limbs onto rooflines and cars. Whatever brought the tree down, the order of work stays the same when a storm hits Austell: structures first. The trunk through the roof, the limb pinning a vehicle, the tree blocking the only road out of a low-lying street, all of it ahead of anything cosmetic, with roof tarping to hold a damaged roof until repairs can be made. Once the emergency is stable, the everyday work is right there and available around the clock and year round: tree removal, tree trimming back to safe wood, storm damage cleanup, and stump grinding. You do not have to wait for the creek to rise or the sky to open. Elite Catastrophe works Austell on the calm days and the hard ones alike.
treeAngle: creek-bottom trees uprooting in saturated ground

name: Mableton
narrative: Mableton is one of Georgia's newer cities, incorporated only recently in south Cobb County, though the trees standing over it have been here far longer than the city limits. That mix is the whole story: established older neighborhoods with mature canopy sitting right alongside newer subdivisions full of leftover pines. When a North Georgia storm crosses south Cobb, both kinds of tree fail, and Elite Catastrophe is the crew serving Mableton when the wreckage needs clearing. We come out of Rome, a family-owned storm-response and tree company, and we answer the phone around the clock and year round, driving to wherever the damage is. In the older parts of Mableton, mature oaks and sweetgums over settled neighborhoods drop heavy limbs and split apart under hail and straight-line wind. In the newer subdivisions, the tall pines left standing after construction lean and snap out of saturated red clay. Either way, when a storm hits Mableton the priority holds: structures first. Trees on houses, trees on cars, trees across the road or the driveway, handled ahead of anything that can wait, with roof tarping and storm damage cleanup to close up what the storm opened. That emergency response is what we lead with, because it cannot wait. The rest of the work is no less available: tree removal, tree trimming back to safe wood, and stump grinding, on a clear Tuesday just as readily as after a warning. A homeowner in Mableton does not have to time a hazard tree to a storm. Elite Catastrophe serves the city in full, and the road from Rome stays open every day of the year.
treeAngle: mixed old canopy and leftover south-Cobb pines

name: Dallas
narrative: Ask anyone in Dallas who has been through a rough Paulding County storm season and they can describe the aftermath: driveways blocked, limbs down across the yard, and at least one tree leaning somewhere new. As the Paulding County seat, Dallas has grown quickly, its wooded subdivisions spreading across the red clay hills that define this part of Georgia. All that new construction left tall trees standing close to new houses, and that is a setup storms know how to exploit. The clay is the quiet villain. It holds water long after the rain stops, and a loblolly pine rooted in saturated ground has very little to hang onto when straight-line winds arrive. Over it goes, across a driveway or onto a roof. The big hardwoods fail on their own terms, shedding heavy limbs without warning or splitting apart in a strong gust. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Dallas and the rest of Paulding County. Storm response is where we lead: when severe weather crosses the county, our crews work structures first, clearing the trees that have landed on homes, vehicles, and roads before anything else, with emergency calls answered around the clock. When a tree opens up the roof, we tarp it to keep the weather out until repairs follow. The storm is the headline, but the everyday work stays available year round: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and storm damage cleanup so the yard is whole again. In a fast-growing county seat like Dallas, plenty of calls come with a tree already down, but a leaning pine on a calm afternoon is exactly the one we would rather see first.
treeAngle: Paulding seat subdivisions rooted in red clay

name: Hiram
narrative: Hiram grew up along US-278 as a retail hub for Paulding County, and the neighborhoods spreading out from that busy corridor are full of the pines and hardwoods that come standard in North Georgia. Those trees are pleasant to live under right up until a storm turns them into a hazard, and around Hiram the storms come every season. The physics are always the same. A stretch of heavy rain saturates the red clay, straight-line winds and hail arrive on a hot afternoon, and the tall loblolly pines left standing when the subdivisions went in start to give. Some snap partway up. Others lift out at the roots and fall whole, onto a roof, across a driveway, or down onto the power line. The oaks and other hardwoods add their own damage, dropping limbs heavy enough to crush whatever is beneath them. Elite Catastrophe answers Hiram as a family-owned company based in Rome, serving Hiram and the rest of Paulding County. Storm response leads what we do, worked structures first: the trees that have hit houses, vehicles, and roads get cleared first, with emergency calls answered around the clock and year round whenever the storms decide to move. When a limb drives through the roof, we tarp the opening to keep the rain outside until repairs can be made. Once the danger is off the house and out of the road, the follow-up runs on your timeline: tree removal, tree trimming, stump grinding, and storm damage cleanup. Along the US-278 corridor, many calls come with a tree already down, but the everyday tree work is here year round, and a questionable tree is always worth a look before the next storm.
treeAngle: US-278 hub pines and hardwoods over homes
